card.intuition: Standardisation (z-score): subtract mean, divide by std → N(0,1). Min-max scaling: subtract min, divide by range → [0,1]. Unscaled features with very different magnitudes cause gradient descent to converge slowly (loss contours are elongated ellipses) and distance-based algorithms to ignore small-scale features.
card.analogy: Converting currencies before comparing prices: if one feature is in dollars and another in cents, the model incorrectly treats the cent-feature as 100× more important. Scaling puts everything on the same currency.
card.time_complexity: O(n) per feature for fit and transform
card.space_complexity: O(d) to store mean/std or min/max per feature
when_to_use: Before any gradient-based algorithm (linear/logistic regression, neural networks, SVMs)
Not needed for tree-based models (decision trees, random forests, gradient boosting)
gotchas: Fit scaler on TRAIN data only — apply same transform to validation and test to prevent data leakage
Outliers distort min-max scaling — use standardisation or robust scaler (median/IQR) for outlier-heavy data
Tree-based models are invariant to feature scaling — no need to scale for them

hints: Always fit the scaler on X_train only — fitting on test data would leak test statistics into preprocessing
exercise.test_code:
import math
X_train = [[1,100],[2,200],[3,300],[4,400]]
X_test  = [[2.5, 250]]

ss = StandardScaler()
Xtr, Xte = fit_transform(ss, X_train, X_test)
assert math.isclose(sum(r[0] for r in Xtr)/len(Xtr), 0, abs_tol=1e-9)
assert math.isclose(sum(r[1] for r in Xtr)/len(Xtr), 0, abs_tol=1e-9)
assert math.isclose(Xte[0][0], 0.0, abs_tol=1e-6)  # 2.5 is the mean

mm = MinMaxScaler()
Xtr2, Xte2 = fit_transform(mm, X_train, X_test)
assert Xtr2[0] == [0.0, 0.0]
assert Xtr2[-1] == [1.0, 1.0]
assert Xte2[0][0] == 0.5  # (2.5-1)/(4-1)
print('PASS')
